coredata.dc:title: Preparation of multifunctional PEG-graft-Halloysite Nanotubes for Controlled Drug Release, Tumor Cell Targeting, and Bio-imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Yamina, Ait Mehdi
Fizir, Meriem
Itatahine, Asma
He, Hua
Dramou, Pierre
coredata.dc:description: With the intent of enhancing the loading capacity and controlled the release of a low-water soluble drug (quercetin), 6 ar ms (Poly-Ethylene-Glycol)-amine was grafted in the external surface of halloysite nanotubes. The grafted halloysite nanotubes (HNTs-g-PEG) were decorated with carbon quantum dots for additive fluorescents properties. Conjointly, biotin was conjugated to PEG’s free amine groups for precise targeting of tumor tissue and higher cellular uptake. The obtained nanoparticles were characterized by the FTIR, TEM, XRD, zeta potential and TGA analysis. The photoluminescence (PL) properties were investigated by firstly, observing under UV-light at 365 nm; then, the fluorescence spectra of modified HNTs at different levels of preparation were obtained and showed a suitable blue fluorescence. Furthermore, the fluorescent properties were demonstrated by the optical image of HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin obtained from the confocal microscope, which could be interesting for both in vitro and in vivo imaging. Besides, the prepared NPs showed a superior loading capacity of Que (278.36 mg/g) at optimal adsorption conditions comparing to pristine HNTs. The in vitro drug release from these NPs was relatively sustained and pH sensitive. The incubation of the prepared HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin NPs with HeLa cells showed a low toxicity and a suitable biocompatibility. The MTT assay of the Que-loaded NPs possesses enhanced antitumor activity over the free Que.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Halloysite nanotubes
Poly-(ethylene-glycol) grafting
Carbon dots
Controlled drug release
Tumor cells targeting
Bio-imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: PEG grafting on the surface of HNTs-COOH
Preparation of biotin conjugated HNTs-g-PEG-CDs nanoparticles

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Characteristic optical spectra of CDs photoluminescent spectra. Inset: photographs taken under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right). (B) Emission spectra of CDs recorded for progressively longer excitation wavelengths.
Schematic illustration of the chemical structure and preparation procedure of.
FTIR spectra of HNTs, HNTs-NH2, HNTs-COOH, HNTs-g-PEG (A), TEM images of HNTs-g-PEG with scale bare 100 nm with 20 nm insert in the right bottom (B), and 50 nm (C).
XRD of HNTs and HNTs-g-PEG (A), Zeta potential of HNTs-g-PEG and HNTs-g-PEG-Biotin (B).

TGA (A), DTG (B) of HNTs, HNTs-g-PEG, and HNTs-g-PEG-Biotin HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin, water solutions of CDs, HNTs-g-PEG, HNTs-g-PEG-CDs and HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin were prepared and observed under the bright field and UV light at 365 nm (Fig. 6(A) and (B)).

Photographs of CDs, HNTs-g-PEG, HNTs-g-PEG-CDs, and HNTs-g-CDs-Biotin took under the bright field (A), under UV light at 365 nm, PL spectra of CDs, HNTs-g-PEG, HNTs-g-PEG-CDs and HNTs-g-CDs-Biotin at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm (C), fluorescence optical image 2D (D), 3D (E) of HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin.
Que release from HNTs and HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin (A), Que release from HNTs -g-PEG-CDs-Biotin at pH 7.4, 6.8 and pH 5.0 (B).
In vitro cytotoxicity of HNTs-g-PEG-Biotin towards HeLa cells (A), free Que, Que-loaded HNTs-g-PEG and Que-loaded HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin (B) determined by MTT assay.

head.ce:title: Preparation of multifunctional PEG-graft-Halloysite Nanotubes for Controlled Drug Release, Tumor Cell Targeting, and Bio-imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, School of Sciences, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Drug Quality Control and Pharmacovigilance, Pharmaceutical University, Ministry of Education, 639 Longmian Avenue, Nanjing, 211198, Jiangsu Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Drug Quality Control and Pharmacovigilance

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Analytical Chemistry, School of Sciences, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the intent of enhancing the loading capacity and controlled the release of a low-water soluble drug (quercetin), 6 ar ms (Poly-Ethylene-Glycol)-amine was grafted in the external surface of halloysite nanotubes. The grafted halloysite nanotubes (HNTs-g-PEG) were decorated with carbon quantum dots for additive fluorescents properties. Conjointly, biotin was conjugated to PEG’s free amine groups for precise targeting of tumor tissue and higher cellular uptake. The obtained nanoparticles were characterized by the FTIR, TEM, XRD, zeta potential and TGA analysis. The photoluminescence (PL) properties were investigated by firstly, observing under UV-light at 365 nm; then, the fluorescence spectra of modified HNTs at different levels of preparation were obtained and showed a suitable blue fluorescence. Furthermore, the fluorescent properties were demonstrated by the optical image of HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin obtained from the confocal microscope, which could be interesting for both in vitro and in vivo imaging. Besides, the prepared NPs showed a superior loading capacity of Que (278.36 mg/g) at optimal adsorption conditions comparing to pristine HNTs. The in vitro drug release from these NPs was relatively sustained and pH sensitive. The incubation of the prepared HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin NPs with HeLa cells showed a low toxicity and a suitable biocompatibility. The MTT assay of the Que-loaded NPs possesses enhanced antitumor activity over the free Que.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanosized tubular halloysite also called halloysite nanotube (HNT) in some materials science publications, is the dominant form of naturally occurring halloysite, with a structural formula of Al2(OH)4Si2O5·nH2O [1]. HNTs have been attracting a great interest in the biomedical field [2–4]. The negatively charged outer surface and positively charged inner core of halloysite under most natural pH conditions allow various modifications for using as drug delivery carriers [5,6]. In addition, they possess good biocompatibility, low cost, and environmental friendliness [7]. Therefore, HNTs represent a promising candidate for drug delivery [8,9]. Halloysite nanotubes were successfully employed for several hydrophobic drugs encapsulation [10–13]. Generally, HNTs are modified by different polymers for sustained drug release. The polymer modified-HNTs nanocomposites have shown many potential applications in drug and DNA delivery [14].

Polyethylene glycol (PEG) as a synthesized polymer is commonly used in biomedicine due to its excellent hydrophilicity, biocompatibility, and biodegradability [15,16]. Generally, the nanoparticle’s surface modification with PEG is in the aim of reducing the immunogenicity, toxicity, protein adsorption, surface interaction, undesirable cellular uptake and cell adhesion [17,18]. PEG-grafted to surfaces of biomedical devices proved to increase their biocompatibility and to reduce thrombogenicity [19,20].

Carbon nanomaterials such as graphene oxide (GO) and carbon nanotubes (CNTs), when functionalized with PEG, show enhanced efficiency as drug carriers [21,22]. Recently, some works are focused on PEG surface modification of HNTs. Poly(ethylene) glycol (PEG) 20,000 NPs were filled with clay nanotubes (HNTs). In the plan with representing a new route to develop smart materials used in advanced applications [23]. PEG was non-covalently coated on the surface of HNTs with the aim of improving their biocompatibility which was confirmed on both of HeLa and HepG2 cells. [24], HNTs were conjugated with poly(ethylene glycol) and folate (HNTs-PEG-FA) for doxorubicin targeted delivery to cancer cells [25].

Overexpression of the drug-efflux transporter P-glycoprotein (P-gp) is a key factor contributing to tumor drug resistance. The covalent conjugation of biomolecular probes such as proteins to nanoparticles has led to a novel platform of targeting [26]. Biotin, also known as vitamin H or B7 has a strong and stable affinity for the proteins. Numerous studies have reported that cancer cell overexpress biotin receptors in contrast to the normal cells [27]. Biotin is absorbed mainly through a sodium-dependent multivitamin transporter (SMVT), which is overexpressed in breast, lung, ovarian, and renal cancer cells [28]. Biotin-conjugated nanocarriers not only increase the cellular uptake of drugs but also allow safer and targeted delivery of drugs to tumor tissues.

Carbon dots are known as one of the most important carbon materials. Due to their good water solubility, biocompatibility, low cost, easy surface modification, chemical and photochemical stability and high quantum yield [29]. Recently, many research has been focused on the development of multifunctional fluorescent hybrids based on CDs which could be exploited for bio-imaging [30,28] along with sensing. Consequently, various techniques have been employed for the synthesis of CDs [31,32]. Hydrothermal treatment of carbon precursor is considered as one of the most efficient methods as it is cheap and eco-friendly or green approach. The carbon dots fabricated by this method show a high photostability and high quantum yield [33–35].

In the present work, we propose a novel drug delivery system. By 6-arm PEG-NH2 grafting on the external surface of HNTs, followed by conjugation of biotin as a biological vector for the selective delivery of cytotoxic compounds to the cancer cells. The HNTs-g-PEG was decorated by a synthesized fluorescent carbon dots for cell imaging. It should be noted that is the first report of multi-arms (PEG-NH2) covalently grafting on HNTs, for application in drug delivery field. Quercetin (Que) was chosen as a model drug to observe the loading process by physical adsorption and the release behavior profiles. The anti-tumor activity of the blank HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin, free Que, Que-loaded HNTs-g-PEG and Que-loaded HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin was evaluated by MTT assay against HeLa cells. This novel nanocarrier is proposed as a promising candidate for biomedical applications such as drug delivery, stimuli-responsive drug release, cell bioimaging with the addition of a specific tumor cell targeting.

Halloysite clay was obtained from DanjiangKou, China. 6-arm poly(ethylene glycol)-amine (PEG-NH2) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). quercetin, biotin (Vitamin H (B7)) (C10H16N2O3S), 1-ethyl-3-(-3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), citric acid anhydrous, ethylenediamine, aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), succinic anhydride, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), acetic acid, ethanol and methanol were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). All chemicals and solutions used were of analytical reagent grade. All aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PEG grafting on the surface of HNTs-COOH
ce:section.ce:para: PEG-grafted HNTs (HNTs-g-PEG) were synthesized via EDC chemistry, according to the reported methods of chitosan grafting with convenient modifications [36]. An amount of 2 g HNTs-COOH and 1.5 g EDC were ultrasonicated in 150 mL ultrapure water for 10 min. Subsequently, 1 g NHS and 0.5 g PEG were added to the solution. Afterward, the final mixture was stirred for 12 h, at room temperature. HNTs-g-PEG were obtained by centrifuging, then washed three times with water, and dried at 40℃ in a vacuum.

The CDs grafting on the extremities of 6-arm poly(ethylene glycol)-amine branches was adapted from a reported method with few modifications [37]. First, 1.0 mg/mL of CDs dispersed in water was treated with 10 mg/mL of HNT-g-PEG for 10 min. After that, 10 mM of EDC were added, and the resulting mixed solution was sonicated for 1 h, followed by adding EDC (20 mM) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 20 mM), stirring for 24 h. The final reaction solution was further purified by centrifugation (12,000 rpm) for 1 h. Finally, the HNTs-g-PEG-CDs nanoparticles were dried under vaccum at 40 ℃.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of biotin conjugated HNTs-g-PEG-CDs nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The novel biotin conjugated HNTs-g-PEG-CDs was synthesized, following the reported method with some modifications [38]. Firstly, the biotin (230 mg) was activated using EDC (160 mg) in DMSO (10 mL) by stirring this mixture at room temperature for 1 h. Then the activated biotin was added to the HNTs-g-PEG-CDs solution (400 mg) dissolved in sodium acetate buffer (pH 4.5). The mixture was stirred for 24 h at room temperature. Finally, the biotin conjugate HNTs-g-PEG-CDs NPs were dialyzed (molecular weight cut off 2000 Da) against phosphate buffer (pH 8.0) for 48 h in order to remove the excess of biotin, EDC, and DMSO. The final solution was dried at 40℃under vacuum for further use.

Firstly, 1 mg of Que were dissolved in 2 mL of methanol. Besides, 5 mg HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin were dissolved in 8 mL of water and added to the Que solution. After stirring for 12 h, the final Que@HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin was obtained by centrifuging at 4000 rpm for 10 min, then washed once with methanol and once with ultrapure water to remove the non-loaded Que. The supernatant liquid was collected to measure the absorbance. The adsorption capacity of Que was calculated using an ultraviolet spectrophotometer and evaluated by Eq. (1):
(1) Adsorption capacity (mg/g) = (W−C*V)/M
Where W is the mass of Que used for drug loading, mg; C is the concentration of Que in the supernatant, mg/mL; V is the volume of the filtrate, mL; M is the mass of halloysite used for drug loading, g.

Batch kinetic experimental data for adsorbed Que onto HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin and HNTs were analyzed using pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order. The equilibrium experimental data were analyzed using Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models. (view Supporting information)

Que release from loaded samples was carried out in phosphate buffer saline with different pH values via a common method. Normal physiological conditions were simulated by a pH 7.4, the extracellular and intracellular conditions were simulated by pH 6.8 and pH 5.0 respectively. For release experiments, certain amounts of Que loaded nanotubes were immersed in 100 mL buffer solutions and stirred at 100 rpm at 37℃. At specified time intervals, 4 mL of the solution was taken at the same time and the same amounts of buffer solutions were added to fix the overall volume in constant value. The removed samples were analyzed by a UV–vis spectrophotometer to evaluate the Que concentration.
The kinetics theoretical models were applied to further study the release behavior and the release mechanism of Que from the nanotubes. (Supporting information)

The cytotoxic effects of the blank HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin NPs, free Que, Que-loaded HNTs-g-PEG and Que-loaded HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin NPs on HeLa cells were evaluated by MTT assay. The cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 1 × 104 cells per well. After incubation for 24 h, the culture medium was removed and fresh DMEM containing serial concentrations of the above NPs (convenient NPs concentrations to get the Que concentrations) (00.1, 1.0, 10, 25, 50, 100 μg/mL) in PBS formulations were added. After an incubation of 24 h, 50 μL of the MTT solution (2 mg/mL MTT in PBS, pH 7.4) was added to each well and the plate was incubated for further 4 h. After incubation, the culture medium containing MTT was removed by rinsing with fresh PBS. Then the intracellular formazan crystals were extracted by adding 150 μL of DMSO into the plate. The absorbance was measured at 570 nm using a microplate reader (SpectraMax M3, Molecular Devices, USA).

The morphology of the synthesized NPs was observed by an FEI Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope (TEM). Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometric (FT-IR) spectra were measured on an 8400 s FT-IR spectrometer using KBr pellets (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan). Fluorescence spectra of the prepared CDs were measured on a Shimadzu RF-5301 (PC) S (Japan) spectrofluorometer at room temperature. Fluorescent images were investigated using a Carl Zeiss Jena LSM700 (Germany) confocal laser scanning microscope; the samples were prepared by dispersing the microspheres in ultrapure water then spin coated onto glass microscope slides, and the photographs were taken with a Canon digital camera of suspensions of different materials in bright field and under UV light using a ZF-90 UV transilluminator (SH1717, China). Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of the samples were recorded by a UV-1800 spectrometer. TGA was carried out by Mettler-Toledo TGA/DSC3+. The temperature was controlled from room temperature to 800℃ at a heating rate of 10℃/min under the N2 atmosphere, XRD.

The highly photoluminescent CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal reaction of citric acid as the carbon source with ethylenediamine for nitrogen doping (view supporting information for the detailed method).

The optical properties were investigated by dissolving the CDs in aqueous solution. They showed a strong absorption at 340 nm (Fig.1 A). Upon excitation at the peak absorption wavelength of 340 nm at room temperature, the CDs exhibited a photoluminescence (PL) spectrum with a maximum of 440 nm. Along with the changed excitation from 340 to 500 nm the photoluminescence peak shifted from 440 to 540 nm with dramatically intensity decreasing (Fig.1B). The decrease in PL-intensity can be ascribed to a decrease in the absorption of increasing wavelengths [39].

HNTs-g-PEG was synthesized by firstly activating the surface of HNTs with APTES, then the obtained HNTs-NH2 was treated with succinic anhydride to convert amine to a carboxylic group. Followed by, chemical coupling of HNTs-COOH and 6-arm poly(ethylene glycol)-amine via EDC/NHS chemistry (view supporting information for the detailed method). The as-prepared HNTs-g-PEG was decorated with the prepared CDs, by EDC/NHS coupling chemistry. Finally, the PEG-CDs modified HNTs was conjugated by biotin in the free PEG-NH2 branches via EDC method. The successful preparation and modifications of the final HNTs were confirmed by TEM, TGA, FTIR, zeta-potential, fluorescence microscopy and UV-light absorption at a different level of preparation. Fig.2 . illustrate different steps of HNTs modification and the chemical structure of the final HNTs.

The microstructural changes of the HNTs samples referring to the different level of surface modification were determined by FTIR. Fig.3 (A) shows the FTIR spectrum of HNTs, HNTs-NH2, HNTs-COOH, and HNTs-g-PEG. In the pristine HNTs spectra, the peaks rising at 3695 and 3620 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of the Al−OH bonds, as well as in-plane Si–O stretching at 1091 cm-1 and 1031 cm-1 [40].

Successful aminopropyl functionalization of HNTs was confirmed by the absorption peaks at 2950 cm−1 and 1573 cm−1, corresponding to CH and NH bond respectively. An apparent peak at 1653 cm−1 corresponds to the carboxylic group, while the peaks around 2950 cm-1 disappear by meaning that NCO was turned into COOH [36]. In the HNTs-g-PEG spectrum, 2928 cm-1 peak corresponds to the stretching vibrations of the CH2−. More outstandingly, the peaks at 1657 and 1567 cm-1 were assigned to the CN stretching vibration and NH bending vibrations, respectively of 6-arms (PEG-NH2) [41].

The characteristic peaks of pure HNTs were shown in all the spectra at different modification levels, signifying that the basic crystal structures of HNTs were not changed after the modifications. The morphological structure of HNTs-g-PEG NPs was observed by TEM (Fig. 3(C) and (D)).

The HNTs as a raw material possessed an external diameter of 50–60 nm, a lumen diameter of 12–20 nm, and a length of 1 μm (Fig.3B) [3]. TEM images of HNTs-g-PEG (Fig.3C–D) reveal three areas with different transparency to the electrons and in the case of the raw HNTs reveal only two area. From outside to inside of HNTs-g-PEG images it is possible to distinguish a region of low opacity that may be attributed to the 6-arm-PEG-NH2 layer coating the HNTs surface, followed by a darker area, related to the rolled aluminosilicate sheets and finally the inner lumen of the tube (more visible), with shell thickness between 20 and 26 nm. As known X-ray diffraction (XRD) is a useful method to inspect the crystallinity of materials, Fig. 4 (A) shows the XRD pattern spectrums for HNTs and HNTs-g-PEG.

Apparent peaks at 12°, 20° and 25° were observed on HNTs spectrum. Which are referred by the crystalline structure of typical silicate materials [42,43]. In the HNTs-g-PEG spectrum, most of the characteristic peaks of HNTs appears only with lower intensity. Nevertheless the reflection of these peaks implying that the crystal structures of HNTs were not significantly changed. The biotin was conjugated on the PEG free branches after decoration of HNTs-g-PEG by CDs. As known biotin is positively charged contrarily to PEG and CDs. To confirm the biotin conjugation on the HNTs-g-PEG-CDs surface, the zeta potential of both HNTs-g-PEG-CDs and HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin was measured (Fig. 4(B)). The value of zeta potential of HNTs-g-PEG was -21.81 ± 2.1 and +37.44 ± 3.2 for biotin conjugated HNTs-g-PEG-CDs. While, in biotin grafted NPs DTG graph, two small weight loss events at were carried out. Fig. 5 (A and B) represent TGA and derivative thermogravimetric (DTG) curves of HNTs, HNTs-g-PEG, and HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin. Thermal analysis of the modified HNTs under a nitrogen atmosphere in the temperature range of 30–800 ℃ and with a 10 ℃/min heating rate.

From DTG graph, it can be seen that raw HNTs and HNTs-g-PEG exhibited a peak at about 525 ℃ and 520 ℃ which covered a temperature range at a maximum rate of 80 and 180 ℃, respectively, and more important peaks at 300 and 470 ℃ were noted.

The TGA graphs have shown that HNTs-g-PEG-Biotin have more weight loss than HNTs-g-PEG and raw HNTs from 100∼800 ℃. Furthermore, for HNTs-g-PEG have more weight loss than HNTs from 300∼800 ℃. The weight loss of HNTs is due to the hydroxyl group dehydration of structural aluminol groups [44], although in both HNTs-g -PEG and HNTs-g-PEG-Biotin case the weight loss is not only attributed to the dehydration but also the PEG and biotin degradation. Since the weight of CDs is negligible compared to 6 ar ms (PEG-NH2) and biotin weight, the weight loss of CDs was not considered in this analysis. The PEG grafting density was found about 3.4%.

To investigate the PL properties of the synthesized HNTs-g-PEG-CDs and As shows photographs in Fig. 6 both of CDs, HNTs-g-PEG-CDs and HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin exhibit a strong and stable blue PL, contrary to the HNTs-g-PEG. Furthermore, the PL spectra (Fig. 6(C)) of CDs, HNTs-g-PEG-CDs and HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin demonstrate apparent emission peak with an excitation wavelength of 340 nm when HNTs-g-PEG spectra show a negative result. These results indicate that the CDs were successfully linked to the PEG branches, and after the biotin conjugation the PL properties remained intact. The photoluminescent properties of the prepared HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin were furthermore investigated by confocal laser scanning microscope (Fig. 6D and E).
The PL optical image of the prepared HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin shows a strong photoluminescence observed at 340 nm, suitable for in vitro and in vivo imaging.

For the Que adsorption conditions optimization results view Fig. S1 and the Que adsorption onto the modified HNTs follows the pseudo-second-order model. The regression analysis, corresponding to Langmuir and Freundlich models indicate that the two adsorption processes of Que onto HNTs and HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin were following Langmuir model. (Fig. S2 and Table S2)

Firstly, Que was released from both HNTs and HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin NPs in PBS solution at pH 7.4 (Fig. 7 (A)). In the second place, it was released from HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin in PBS solutions at pH 6.8 and pH 5.0 and compared to the release carried out at pH 7.4 (Fig. 7(B)).

The Que release behavior from HNTs both, HNTs and HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin NPs was similar during the 10 first hours. That from HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin becomes slower with the total amount of 44.20% after 7 days, when the release from the raw HNTs was 70.92%. The low rate of release observed from the HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin NPs is related to the sustained Que release behavior.
It is due to the presence of the strong π−π interaction between Que and PEG branches.

As shown Fig. 7(B), the Que release was slower in PBS pH 7.4 than pH 6.8 and pH 5.0. After 7 days 44.20% of Que was released at pH 7.4 when Que released at pH 6.8 and pH 5.0 reached 67.92% and 75.43% respectively. This difference of Que release rate from HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin at different pH is due to the presence of hydrogen bond between Que and free PEG-NH2 branches at pH 7.4, which can be dissociated at acidic pH. Also, the faster Que release rate at acidic pH is due to the higher Que solubility at these pH than pH 7.4.

The results from the statistical analysis of Que release from the raw and the modified HNTs (Table S3) indicate that the Que release mechanism from raw HNTs is non-Fickian diffusion, which is not controlled by an interaction. Contrary to the Que release from HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin, it can be controlled by the interactions (such as π−π and hydrogen bound interaction) between Que and PEG.

The anticancer effect of the free Que, blank HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin, HNTs-g-PEG-Que and HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin-Que on HeLa cells was carried out by MTT assay. Preliminary cytotoxicity evaluation assay of empty HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin was performed with the concentration range of NPs between 0.01 and 100 μg/mL for 24 h. It can be seen in Fig.8 (A) that the blank nanoparticles containing HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin showed cell viability higher than 80% even at increased concentrations. These results suggest that the prepared nanoparticles possess low toxicity, as well as significant safety concern in vitro and in vivo applications, which is in agreement with the literature results about the cytotoxicity of these materials (HNTs, PEG, CDs, and Biotin) [8,45,46].

Fig. 8(B) shows that the Que-loaded HNTs-g-PEG exhibited a similar reduction of viability of the HeLa cells as free Que after 24 h of incubation. The cell viability at higher concentration (10 μg/mL) for the Que-loaded HNTs-g-PEG was 29.33%, while the free Que exhibited cell viability of 34.52%. The gradually release thereby, exposed the cells to the drug for a prolonged period [47].

Finally, the tumor targeting capability of Que-loaded HNTs-g-PEG-Biotin was evaluated by the MTT assay. Remarkably, these NPs exhibited a more significant anticancer effect compared with that of free Que and Que-loaded HNTs-g-PEG NPs. The results proved that the biotin-conjugated HNTs-g-PEG not only maintained the pharmacological action of Que but also enhanced its anticancer effect. The greater cytotoxic effect of Que-loaded HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin may be attributed to the higher cellular uptake and controlled release of Que in the intracellular environment [48].

In summary, a multifunctional HNTs with targeting and imaging abilities were successfully synthesized and characterized. By, firstly linking covalently 6 ar m-poly (ethylene-glycol) to HNTs external surface, confirmed by FTIR and XRD analysis followed by TEM observation. Suitable photoluminescent properties were achieved by a biocompatible carbon dots conjugation into the PEG-NH2 branches of HNTs-g-PEG NPs for cancer cells imaging. The obtained HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin exhibited a superior loading capacity regarding quercetin anticancer agent. In addition to, a sustained and pH-sensitive drug release from the modified HNTs compared to the pristine ones. Further analysis of Que release from raw and modified HNTs confirmed the presence of diffusion controlled release process and the presence of interactions between Que and HNTs-g-PEG-CDs-Biotin, unlike the raw HNTs. Moreover, the prepared NPs exhibited a low toxicity and high biocompatibility as well as an increased anti-tumor effect when loaded with Que over than that of free Que against HeLa cells. The PEG grafted HNTs and decorated with CDs and Biotin nanoparticles are promising to be used as carriers for targeted delivery of drugs and could be tracked by the imaging ability in both in vitro and in vivo.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2018.06.042.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Properties and applications of halloysite nanotubes: recent research advances and future prospects
Appl. Clay Sci.
Halloysite and chitosan oligosaccharide nanocomposite for wound healing
Acta Biomater.
A gelatin composite scaffold strengthened by drug-loaded halloysite nanotubes
Mater. Sci. Eng. C Mater. Biol. Appl.
Halloysite Clay nanotubes for loading and sustained release of functional compounds
Adv. Mater.
Halloysite nanotubes as a new drug-delivery system: a review
Clay Miner.
Nanohydrogel formation within the halloysite lumen for triggered and sustained release
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces
Effect of morphology and size of halloysite nanotubes on functional pectin bionanocomposites for food packaging applications
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces
Multifunctional halloysite nanotubes for targeted delivery and controlled release of doxorubicin in-vitro and in-vivo studies
Nanotechnology
Surface functionalized halloysite nanotubes decorated with silver nanoparticles for enzyme immobilization and biosensing
